name: Daeva
description: Daevas are malevolent entities in Zoroastrianism, often considered demons or false gods. They represent chaos, evil, and the antithesis of Ahura Mazda, the supreme god of Zoroastrianism. In the Gathas, the oldest texts of the Avesta, the Daevas are beings who are misguided and choose the path of Druj, the Lie, rather than Asha, the Truth and Righteousness. They are associated with destructive forces and are actively opposed by the Amesha Spentas, the benevolent divine beings who serve Ahura Mazda. Over time, the concept of the Daevas evolved and became more demonized, with specific Daevas being associated with various vices and sins. They are seen as tempters, leading humans astray and hindering their spiritual progress. The Daevas are not inherently powerful in their own right but derive their strength from human adherence to falsehood and wickedness. Fighting against the Daevas is a central theme in Zoroastrian ethics, urging followers to choose good over evil and to actively resist the forces of darkness.
subtitle: Malevolent entities in Zoroastrianism
appearance: Daevas are not typically described with a fixed appearance in Zoroastrian texts. Their appearance is often assumed to be monstrous or grotesque, reflecting their evil nature. Some accounts depict them as shadowy figures or beings of darkness.
habitat: The realm of darkness and chaos, or locations corrupted by evil.
behavior: Daevas are driven by a desire to undermine the cosmic order established by Ahura Mazda. They seek to corrupt humans, spread lies, and instigate conflict. Their behavior is characterized by deceit, violence, and a general disregard for morality.

keyMyths.title: The Choice Between Asha and Druj
keyMyths.description: This foundational myth highlights the Daevas' choice to follow Druj (the Lie) instead of Asha (Truth), leading to their condemnation as malevolent beings. This choice sets the stage for the cosmic battle between good and evil.

keyMyths.title: Combatting the Daevas
keyMyths.description: Zoroastrian texts emphasize the constant struggle against the Daevas, requiring humans to actively resist their influence through righteous actions, prayer, and adherence to the principles of Asha. This is a central theme in Zoroastrian ethics.

keyMyths.title: Specific Daevas and Their Vices
keyMyths.description: Later Zoroastrian traditions identify specific Daevas with particular vices, such as Aeshma (wrath) and Akoman (evil thought). Stories illustrate how these Daevas tempt humans and lead them astray, emphasizing the importance of vigilance and moral strength.

keyMyths.title: The Role of Angra Mainyu
keyMyths.description: Angra Mainyu, the destructive spirit and ultimate source of evil, is closely associated with the Daevas. He is the embodiment of all that is opposed to Ahura Mazda and leads the Daevas in their battle against good. Angra Mainyu's influence is a constant threat to the world.

sources.author: Boyce, Mary

sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Zoroastrianism, including detailed information on the Daevas and their role in the religion's cosmology.
